speaker: interviewer
text: --interview with Irving Rubenstein. June 1, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russian-- when the Russians start pushing, the front changed. By Stalingrad, they start pushing towards Poland. Then the trouble start to begin. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] As the Russian army moved into Poland, the borders changed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the Polish-- when the Russian army came closer and closer to Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they ordered the Polish people to make a revolt against the Nazis, against the Germans in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the camp at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I were in the camp, still in Kielce. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that the Russians were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, what people told us, from underground newspapers, trains that went through the city. People used to come from Warsaw, people who listened, mostly, from British news, British news, British radio. Polish people had radios in the basement and used to listen from London the news at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make a difference to you that the Russians were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in giving us hope. We figured we lived by hour. There is always hope. It was a chance to survive. The closer they came, the more hope we had-- had. So while the Russian armies came into Poland, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Russian people gave an order to the Polish underground to revolt against the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while-- as the Polish people were revolted, the Russian army stopped and waited till the Germans should kill off the intelligence. And then they would march in. And that's what happened. The Russian army stopped for several week, Hitler took action, start transporting the Polish intelligence, the Polish intellectuals, the respectable people from the cities, the mayors, the chief of police. They started to liquidate the Polish intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this make a difference to you? Were people that you knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't make no difference to me, but those were the atrocities of the war, you know, as the war went on. And they started shipping people more, and more, and more to Auschwitz. And then the Russian army pushed towards Warsaw. While the army pushed towards Warsaw, one morning we woke up-- this was sometime late in 1944. I was still living in Kielce, in the camp. Life wasn't so bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had it changed at all, as a result of the Russians coming? Food, illness, anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't change too much for us, but the hope start to come back that maybe, someday, we liberated. It gave us a little hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it change what you do-- what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure. Sure. It changed a little. We had been planning to escape to the forest. Everybody had a gun. Everybody had a grenade. We had civilian clothing to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get a gun and a grenade in a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We bought from civilian people. Polish people used to do business with the Germans. And the Germans used to sell it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you pay them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a little money hidden. Everybody had something hidden. Yeah. Yeah. So-- and life was going on, and going on, and going on like this. And one morning, we almost were prepared-- if-- if they would have let us alone another week, we would have escaped to the forest. One morning-- we woke up at 4:00 in the morning-- the camp was surrounded by SS. Oh, surrounded. And then thousands of them came. Gave an order to the chief of police that the Russian army is arriving, and we have to evacuate. We've got to go to Germany, to other camps. Two hours later-- we had our own ramp in the factory. They put in boxcars. And we were, in the two camps, probably 900 people. They put us in the boxcars and on the way we go. Two hours later, we left Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the people from the camp, the two camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you with friends of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who worked in the camp. Huta Ludwikow and Huta Henrykow-- we had two factories in the city. They loaded us up, all, in the boxcars. And two hours later, we were on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were awakened at 4:00 in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: 4:00 in the morning, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody dress--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had on a suit. We had a little working suit. Clothing-- we didn't have much. Food-- we didn't have nothing. So we put on the suit, we had a towel, and that's all to get. And then-- and they put us in the boxcars, in the cattle cars, and about two hours later, we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody was wondering and guessing, where are we going? Some said-- the-- the people who ran our camps said that we were going deep into Germany. But when they put us on the train, after four, five hours of traveling, we already felt that we are going the wrong direction. How did you determine that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were people who used to live in other cities in our camp. And they knew the direction, which way we going. Soon we went from Kielce on the way to Kalish. A-- a city, Kalish. K-A-L-I-S-H. Kalish. So he says, if we're in Kalish, we're not going to Germany, we're going to Auschwitz. We already felt. Overnight, we stayed in the railroad all night. The next morning, we arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you say you stayed in a railroad station--

speaker: interviewee
text: Overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean? You stayed in the boxcar?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the boxes. No food, no water, no nothing. We stayed like this for maybe a day and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the boxcar?

speaker: interviewee
text: 900 People. The whole camp. 900, 1,000. The two camps from Kielce-- [? Huta ?] Ludwikow and Henrykow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in the boxcar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horrible. Terrible. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it smell?

speaker: interviewee
text: It smelled. No water, no toilets, no nothing. Suffering, real suffering. And we didn't know what was happening. Then when arrived in the camp-- oh, the real life begins. They were waiting for us with dogs, with machine guns, with all kinds of equipment. They gave us a real welcome there. A real, real welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you see? When you think about when they opened the doors of your car, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw hell. You saw heaven, we saw hell. We knew already that from there, nobody gets out alive. We knew already. We got the message when we arrived there. Anyways, we arrived there, they unloaded us, and they took us to the crematorium. All the way to the crematorium. Everybody got a shower. Everybody washed. And they segregated us. Young people in one side, old people on the other side, women on another side. Children-- there weren't too many children. So anyways, we were segregated and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took us in. From the 900 people-- I don't know how many-- maybe 800 of us, they took us into the barracks. They took us into the-- no, no, no. First, everybody got undressed. They took away all the clothing. Everybody should give them the diamond, the gold, all the belongings, what we had. Everybody-- we didn't have nothing anyways. So we went out naked from there. They gave us uniforms, special uniforms. They dressed us up, and they took us to the barracks. I came to barrack nine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Others went to other barracks. Next day, they talk us out and they gave everybody a number. Me, they gave B3303. And everybody got a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they-- what did they do when they did that? Did they take your name? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took the name--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the process?

speaker: interviewee
text: The address, the birth, and the city, and everything. They kept records on everything. If you were alive now, and they kill you an hour later, they hold record on everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who kept the records?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis, the Germans kept records of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know that they were German at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp-- the-- the managers of the camps were prisoners, German prisoners, but the Nazis kept the records, all the records. Yeah. So they gave everybody a number. And after the number, it was finished, it started, next day, a segregation. They put out-- so it wasn't only the Kielce people there, but people from a neighboring city near Kielce. There were ammunition factories-- Starachowice, Skarzysko, Blyzin, and other camps near-- us, where the people-- everybody got a number and they start segregating.

speaker: interviewer
text: So certain people, they put to the left. Certain people, they put to the right. Certain people, they marched away. And they start loading people to different camps, to labor camps. They had been shipping them, by truck, to Germany. When I came to the segregation, he looked at me, he looked at me, and they put me into a barrack seven. And that's it. Others went to-- to-- to camps. 10 trucks to this camp, five truck to this camp, six truck to this camp. I went into barrack seven. But when I went into barrack seven, somebody spotted me who was in Auschwitz already a long time. He says, Irving, Irving, this is you? I say, yes. He hugged me. He kissed me. He says, you are in a barrack that you will go to the crematorium tonight. In five hours from today, this barrack will be marched in the crematorium. I said, how do you know? You see, all those others on the trucks are going to the camps, to labor camps to work in Germany. And this barrack will go to the crematorium tonight. I say, what do you want me to do? He says, you go in the back, is an open door. And stick out your head. If nobody's around there, when [? you look ?] around, when you trucks loading people, go in line. You will go to work in a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five minute later, I went to the back of the barrack-- my barrack seven was loaded. This barrack had room for 500 people. We must have had 1,500 people in this barrack. So crowded, you couldn't breathe. I went in the back, opened up the-- the door, gave a look, was quiet, I made 10 steps. And they were loading a truck. I was the first one to jump in the truck. They didn't even count me. I was already on the truck. I was young, peppy, strong. One jump. I touch the truck, and I was on the truck. An hour later, the truck left and we went to a labor camp. Several hours later, we arrive-- arrived in a camp. It was called Jaworzno. J-A-W-O-R-Z-N-O, Jaworzno. I arrived there. They had there about 6,000, 7,000 people in the camp. And those people, mostly, were marched every day to work in the coal mines, in the Silesia. Silesia coal mines. Two days later, I went to work, for the first time, in a coal mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was 1944? 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- late 1944. Oh, let's say, late September. I went there, to the coal mines. And I worked in the coal mines. Actually, I didn't work too hard in the coal mines. When I arrived in the coal mines, I spoke German. I met there some German people underground, which liked me very much. I appealed to them. I was one of their boys. We talked a little politic. They gave me a little to eat, but not direct. Once in awhile, I used to get a newspaper. Not direct. They left it in a dark room on a box where they used to keep the tools. But I was the only one that I used to go there with them, so that meant that was for me. So whenever I went there and something was laying there, I took it. So I knew, roughly, who helped me there. There were two people who helped me a little bit. Not too much because they didn't have much either. So-- but anyways, whatever they did for me was good. I worked there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not too hard, I didn't work. I knew how to escape and not to kill myself. I took life easy. I always thought, I wait for tomorrow-- to be strong for tomorrow, not to work for the Germans today. Most people loaded their coals. I worked in the station, yeah, bringing in empty boxcars, shipping out empty boxcars, hanging numbers. I didn't work too hard there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that job? You were new.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave it to me, alone. They gave it to me, alone. You know, I was pushy. I pushed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask-- they asked, who knows how to work machines? Me. Whatever they said, it was me. I went for anything. So I worked there, in this coal mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The life wasn't too good in this-- in this camp, Jaworzno. Life was very, very hard. At night, you worked. In the day time, you slept. You didn't see nobody. You had no contact with nobody. Over there, I didn't know too much already. But in the mine, I collect it, a little information. In the mine, I used to collect. But when I used to come to the camp, I didn't tell too much. We had spies, too many spies, so I kept it to myself. I worked there, in the mine, till 1945. Roughly, January the 15th. January the 15th, the Russian army crossed the Vistula. And in three days, they hit the borders of Schlesien, and they went into Germany. And while they went into Germany, they took us, in groups of 1,500, of 2,000, and marched us by foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans or the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The German marched us by foot from Jaworzno to Gleiwitz. It took us several days, by foot, to get there. They marched us by foot. On the way, lots of people fell. They shot them to death. They never even reached Gleiwitz. I was pretty powerful, strong. After several days, I reached Gleiwitz. We arrived in Gleiwitz and we went to the railroad station. And they wanted to put us in boxcars and ship us to deep Germany for labor camps to work. But when we arrived in Gleiwitz, the station been bombarded. Every two hours, every three hours, they bombarded the station. They knocked out the station, and they couldn't put us on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there. We laid there, on the ground. It was winter. Snow, wet, frost. No food, no water, nothing. Whatever somebody could organize, found something, but was-- they didn't give us nothing. We stayed there a day or two. And they brought together maybe 20,000 people from all over Schlesien. And they put us on a march to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: The march went from Gleiwitz to Blechhammer. This was the most famous march of my life. As we marched, the winter was very heavy. No stockings, no clothing, no food, no water, no nothing. We marched from Gleiwitz to Blechhammer. I don't know how many mile it is, but it's a pretty good distance. I would say 100, 150 miles. While we marched there-- in the march, where-- I would say there were between 20,000 and 30,000 people-- every few minutes, you could see five people dead, six people dead, seven people. They collapsed, they killed them, and left them. And we would be marching, and marching, and marching, and marching. After several days, we arrived in Blechhammer. We came to Blechhammer. There was an empty camp. Already the Jews from there were shipped to deep Germany. They put us into the camp. We stayed overnight. The next morning, they put the camp on fire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They started shooting in fire bullets from every side of the camp. The camp must have had several thousand people. And they were German prisoners. German people, criminals, were there in the camp. They used to run the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in charge. And they didn't care who they killed. They killed everybody. While I was sleeping in bed, my next door neighbor, my boyfriend, got a bullet in the rear. And after five minutes, he was dead. I left him there, and I said to myself, what are we going to do? I jumped down from the bed, ran out in the back of the camp-- the shooting came from the front and from the side. And in the back, there was a field-- a big, huge field. And I saw some Germans there, knocking a hole in the wall. They had hammers. I say, fellows, what are you doing? And they say to me, look, they will kill till the last one. This camp will be gone in the next two, three hour. This is the only way to escape. They knocked out a hole in the back of the wall. And the fire almost hit us, and we still had a chance. Maybe 100, 150 people escaped. We ran out. Where can we go? We didn't know where to go. I went into an empty house where there were no Germans, two, three kilometers away. All I knew that I was not far away from two rivers. I don't remember the name of the rivers, but I think I was either near the Oder or near the Neisse. Oder or Neisse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I escaped, I went into the German house. There was a radio and I listened to the radio right away. And they said that the Russians are almost by the Oder and the Neisse. And there's a possibility that tomorrow, they cross the rivers-- that they will blow up the bridges and cross the rivers. No, no. They said that they may cross, but the Germans may blow up the bridges. Those people who were with me were from the vicinity. Those criminals, they knew which way to go. They went to one side and two days later, they were liberated. And I went to the wrong side of the river. And I stayed in the house several days. Later, I came out and nobody was there, but I saw the evacuation of the Germans from Europe, going with horses, and buggies, with the clothing, with the furniture. They had been chasing them across the river, into deep Germany. I said, oh, I'll follow those Germans. I took a little food, a little water, I helped them with the children. You know, I been one of theirs. I knew German, been-- and I wander, wander with them for several days. Till we came-- I want to go to Czechoslovakia. I had a map already. And I knew how to go to Czechoslovakia. I heard that the Allied armies are already in Czechoslovakia, and I wasn't too far away from Czechoslovakia. I took a tramwaj. In Europe, there are no buses. In Europe, goes tramwajs go on electricity. Electric trains are tramwajs. I was in a city, Waldenburg. And travel to a city, Leoben. L-E-O-B-E-N. Leoben. This is some place on the border of Czechoslovakia, but 30, 40 kilometer from Waldenburg. And there, I figured, I would sneak through the border and I be free. While I arrived in this city on the Czechoslovakian border, from there, the Germans put the Germans on trains and sent them to deep Germany. But you had to have a passport. I didn't have no password, so they knew I was a Jew. They took me and put me in a camp in Waldenburg. And this was possible in March, late March. They put me in the city of Waldenburg. There was a camp. And they brought me into the camp and put me on-- in the camp clothing. And there were, maybe, 800 people, 700 people. And I stayed in the camp till the rest of the war, till the liberation, 1945. It was a short time already. It was almost March. It was the 15th of March. While I was in the camp, they took me to work. Nearby, was a factory-- AG [INAUDIBLE]. It was a huge, German factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we want to make barricade, that the-- the-- the Russians shouldn't be able to arrive there. So we went and made those [INAUDIBLE], you know, for a few weeks. But nothing helped. The war was going on there. The sky was on fire, day and night, day and night. We saw that it was almost near the end. Too much trouble, they didn't give us in the camp, those Germans, they, of course, they knew it was near the end. So I stayed in this camp till May the 8th, May the 8th. The Russian tank arrived, broke the door, and said, brothers, you are free. Go wherever you want. And I went out from the camp. This was the end. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? I had swollen feet, I weigh 80 pound. I couldn't walk too much, so I stayed there maybe for two weeks. I went into houses, I organized a little food, the Russians gave me a little soup every day. I went to the Russians. Wherever, you know, they have a station, I went in. And I said, look, see here, I'm hungry. They gave me bread, a little food. I hung around there for two weeks. Where can you go? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I went back to the place where I was born. I say, the only way is to go back to Kielce to look for another brother of another Jew. So [DRINKS] there were no trains, [DRINKS] no transportation. I went on the highway. I had a tiny, little suitcase. I didn't have much. And I was hitchhiking. So a Russian truck passed by, I said, where are you going? He was going to Czestochowa. I said, Czestochowa? I said, fine for me. Will you take me with? He says, yes. He took me on the truck. He took me to Czestochowa. Czestochowa is in Poland. I came to Czestochowa. I didn't have a penny in the pocket. Nothing. I found out that in Czestochowa, there was already a Jewish community. I went to the Jewish community. They gave me a few cent. They gave me something to eat. I stayed there for two days, then I bought myself a ticket. I went on the train, and I went to Kielce. I went home to Kielce. I came to Kielce. I walked down the train. I asked somebody-- I said, any Jews in the city here? He says, no. I ask another guy. No. Till I ask a policeman. I said, is there any Jewish community here? [SPEAKING GERMAN]? I said, anybody who will give me help?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you go on Planty 7. There is our Jewish community there. I walked down Planty 7. I came in. The president of the community was a friend of my father. His name was Abraham Czweczarczik. The cook from the kitchen was Goodman Vigodma-- a friend of mine, which I knew from before the war. They give me something to eat. They gave me a few dollar, and I sat there for a couple weeks. I recuperated. And after two or three, my brother arrived. I sit on the balcony, looked down from the third floor, and I noticed my brother came with a friend. My brother came with lots of happiness. We found out who was alive, who was not alive. And that's it. And this was the story so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who survived and who didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother asked me right away, who survived? So I told him. We have a cousin in Skarzysko. I found out that she lives a half an hour from Kielce. And then I knew about another few people. And we settled there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family, we knew that nobody survived. We knew that nobody survived already a long time ago. But we settled there. We stayed in Kielce for several weeks. And we saw that there's no future, no hope. The organizations, the Jewish organizations, started to organize people to leave people. Everybody should go out of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother says, what's the use waiting here? Let's go. So we wanted to go to Germany. We couldn't get the papers for Germany. Somehow we got the papers to Hungary. And from Hungary, we went across to Austria, to Salzburg, Austria. We had some friends there and in Salzburg, Austria, we settled. We stayed there. Were you in a displaced persons camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Salzburg, Austria. In 1945, General Eisenhower came and opened up a military camp for us. And we got together, maybe about 40 or 50 people. And this was our first camp, an American camp. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, it was called Camp Riedenberg. Riedenberg. We stayed there for several weeks. After several weeks, they gave us a section of apartment buildings. Five, six buildings, where they brought together about 600, 700 people from different camps. And over there, we settled. And the name of the camp was Camp New Palestine. It's a fact. From this camp, I got my passport to leave Europe. DP camp, New Palestine, which I have it with me.
